skill.name: defi-registry-manager
skill.description: Guides consistent updates to DeFi token, protocol, chain, network, and RPC registry files across a codebase.
skill.summary: Coordinate DeFi registry changes across frontend, server, shared, and Rust files.
skill.author: BarisSozen

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command execution, network, environment, wallet, C2, and cryptography patterns. Review found these are Markdown tables, checklists, code examples, public token addresses, and RPC configuration templates in SKILL.md, not executable skill code. The remaining publication risk is low because users may be guided to edit RPC environment variables and run grep or curl validation commands.

low_findings.title: Documentation Includes Network and Environment Configuration Examples
low_findings.description: SKILL.md provides RPC URL templates, process.env examples, and curl connectivity checks. This is legitimate DeFi registry guidance, but users should avoid committing real provider keys or secrets when following the examples.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines clearly show documentation examples for RPC endpoints and environment variables. No evidence found that the skill itself reads secrets or sends them to an unauthorized endpoint.

low_findings.title: Validation Commands Are User-Run Shell Examples
low_findings.description: SKILL.md suggests grep and curl commands for local validation. These are not executed by the skill, but marketplace users should review commands before running them in sensitive repositories.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command patterns appear only inside Markdown checklists and fenced examples. The commands are simple validation commands without dynamic command construction or hidden payloads.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dismissed Static Heuristic: Code Execution Plus Network Plus Credential Access
dangerous_patterns.description: The combined heuristic is a false positive in context. The file is a Markdown skill guide with inline paths, RPC placeholders, process.env examples, public token addresses, and validation commands; no executable implementation is present.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Manual review confirmed the flagged constructs are documentation examples. No evidence found of prompt injection, obfuscation, credential exfiltration, or hidden executable code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dismissed Static Heuristic: Wallet and C2 Keywords
dangerous_patterns.description: The flagged wallet and C2 indicators are public Ethereum token contract addresses in a reference table. They are not private keys, seed phrases, command-and-control endpoints, or exfiltration targets.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The table labels each value as a common token address with decimals. These are well-known public contract addresses, not sensitive cryptocurrency wallet credentials.

content.value_statement: DeFi projects often require the same token, chain, and protocol data in several files. This skill provides checklists and templates so registry changes stay complete and consistent.
actual_capabilities: Lists the files that must be updated for tokens, protocols, chains, networks, and admin registry tables.
Provides checklists for adding new tokens, protocols, chains, and RPC network settings.
Supplies TypeScript and Rust examples for token and network registry entries.
Documents common Ethereum token addresses and decimal values for reference.
Suggests validation commands for address consistency and registry coverage.
limitations: Does not automatically inspect or modify repository files.
Does not verify live token metadata or contract addresses by itself.
Assumes the target project has the file layout named in SKILL.md.
Requires users to protect real RPC keys and environment variables.

use_cases.title: Add a New Token Safely
use_cases.description: Use the token checklist to update frontend, Rust, server, and shared decimal registries together.

use_cases.title: Expand Chain Support
use_cases.description: Use the chain and network checklists to add chain IDs, RPC settings, gas parameters, and explorer links.

use_cases.title: Review Registry Consistency
use_cases.description: Use the listed file locations and validation commands to check whether a registry change is complete.

prompt_templates.title: Add a Basic Token
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me add a new ERC-20 token to the DeFi registry. Show each file I need to update and the data I must verify first.

prompt_templates.title: Add a Protocol
prompt_templates.prompt: Guide me through adding a new DEX protocol to the registry. Include frontend, server, schema, and validation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Add Chain Support
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a checklist for adding a new chain to this project, including ChainId types, RPC settings, tokens, and gas configuration.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Registry Pull Request
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my DeFi registry changes for missing files, inconsistent decimals, address casing, RPC configuration gaps, and validation commands to run.

output_examples.input: Add USDC on a new supported chain.
output: A checklist covering address verification, decimals, frontend token list updates, Rust registry updates, wallet service entries, and shared decimal mapping.
A reminder to use checksum addresses and lowercase lookup keys where required.
Validation steps for finding hardcoded references and checking registry consistency.

output_examples.input: Add a new RPC network configuration.
output: A sequence for obtaining an RPC endpoint, adding environment variables, documenting fallback endpoints, and testing connectivity.
Guidance to add explorer URLs, block timing, gas settings, and admin chain metadata.
Warnings to keep real provider keys out of committed files.
A completeness review across protocol constants, router address mappings, arbitrage scanner entries, price oracle adapters, and validation rules.
A concise list of missing or inconsistent registry updates.

best_practices: Verify token addresses, decimals, and checksum formatting before editing registry files.
Update every registry location for the same change in one pull request.
Run validation commands and build checks after changing token, chain, or protocol data.
anti_patterns: Adding a token to only the frontend list while leaving server or Rust registries unchanged.
Committing real RPC provider keys or private environment values to the repository.
Using unverified decimals or non-checksummed addresses for production registry data.

faq.question: Does this skill execute registry updates automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance, templates, and checklists for a human or coding agent to apply.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: Can it verify token addresses on-chain?
faq.answer: No. It tells users to verify addresses, but it does not perform blockchain lookups itself.

faq.question: Why are RPC URLs included?
faq.answer: They are placeholders that show how network configuration should be structured. Real keys must stay private.

faq.question: What project structure does it expect?
faq.answer: It expects frontend, server, shared schema, and Rust core files like the paths named in SKILL.md.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with low risk. The flagged items are documentation examples, but users should review commands and protect secrets.
